title: podman: ping from inside container stalls/fails on third successive run
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

If ping is ran three times in a row from inside a container, it will fail the third time.  If I try again right after the ping failure, it will continue to fail.  If I wait a few seconds before running the command in the reproducer, it will run two times successfully and fail the third time again.  There seems to be some kind of rate limiting?  This happens on several containers but the example below is using busybox.  Running `echo hello` from the container consecutively works regardless of how many times I run it. 

The previous Fedora Atomic Host release (Fedora-AtomicHost-29-20190318.0) did not have this problem.  I isolated the change down to the kernel bump from `kernel 4.20.15-200.fc29 -> 5.0.4-200.fc29`.  The problem goes away if I install the older kernel.  Docker does not have this same issue (see output at the very end).

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  Start up Fedora-AtomicHost-29-20190401.0.  Found here:
https://kojipkgs.fedoraproject.org/compose/updates/Fedora-29-updates-20190401.0/compose/AtomicHost/x86_64/images/Fedora-AtomicHost-29-20190401.0.x86_64.qcow2
2. `for i in {1..3}; do sudo podman run --rm docker.io/busybox ping -c 3 1.1.1.1; sleep 1; done`

**Describe the results you received:**
Ping stops working on third iteration


**Describe the results you expected:**
Ping always works


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
It happens every time.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
$ podman --version
podman version 1.1.2
```

comments.body: Suspicion: overlapping processes (Podman cleanup processes and the fresh Podman processes being run in the loop) are racing, and CNI ends up removing network state necessary for the new container being run. @mccv1r0 is there any locking in CNI that would prevent multiple processes running at the same time from overwriting each other?

Weird I can get this to happen once. Even upping the odds.

 do sudo podman run --rm docker.io/busybox ping -c 1 1.1.1.1; sleep 1; done
@mike-nguyen One thing to try is with a --net=host, to make sure it is the network stack that is causing the issue.  I am pretty sure, but just to make sure.

> Weird I can get this to happen once. Even upping the odds.
> 
> do sudo podman run --rm docker.io/busybox ping -c 1 1.1.1.1; sleep 1; done

@rhatdan You can or can't?

This issue only occurs on `kernel 5.0.4-200.fc29`.  If downgrade to `kernel 4.20.15-200.fc29` it works again.

> Suspicion: overlapping processes (Podman cleanup processes and the fresh Podman processes being run in the loop) are racing, and CNI ends up removing network state necessary for the new container being run. @mccv1r0 is there any locking in CNI that would prevent multiple processes running at the same time from overwriting each other?

@mheon: For e.g. host-local IPAM address allocation, yes, there are locks to serialize access so that containers don't get e.g. the same IP address.
Could this be a kernel bug?

might be in the kernel.  It seems to get confused by the same destination IP address used by the 3 containers.

@mike-nguyen could you try something like:
```
for i in 1.1.1.1 8.8.8.8 9.9.9.9; do sudo podman run --rm docker.io/busybox ping -c 3 $i; sleep 1; done
```
?

> might be in the kernel. It seems to get confused by the same destination IP address used by the 3 containers.

Wouldn't docker have the same issue?

> might be in the kernel. It seems to get confused by the same destination IP address used by the 3 containers.
> 
> @mike-nguyen could you try something like:
> 
> ```
> for i in 1.1.1.1 8.8.8.8 9.9.9.9; do sudo podman run --rm docker.io/busybox ping -c 3 $i; sleep 1; done
> ```
> ?

@giuseppe that worked!

> Weird I can get this to happen once. Even upping the odds.
> 
> do sudo podman run --rm docker.io/busybox ping -c 1 1.1.1.1; sleep 1; done

what kernel are you using?
@teknoraver suggested me that the solution is `sysctl -w net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_icmp_timeout=2`.  Could you try if that solve the issue for you?

I think it works for Docker as it reuses the same IP for the containers, so the entry is still valid.

If it was working on 4.20.x, it was either Podman/CNI also reusing the same IPs, otherwise it is some change in the kernel that we should probably report.  @teknoraver, do you know who is the best contact for reporting this issue?

Me or someone in my team. I'll look into this issue and let you know here on GitHub.

Bye,
Yes, this is the expected behaviour because the conntrack entries didn't expire yet. Try to lower the timeout with `net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_icmp_timeout=X`
Well if it is expected I will close the issue.
